schools.summary: Ellicott studies the annalist's habits — 'forty years' a round number, the queen-mother named, Beersheba a pilgrim sanctuary; Keil surveys the reign's contents and fits Chronicles' additions into the account.
Gill works the synchronism: beginning in Jehu's seventh year, Jehoash reigned some twenty-one or twenty-two years contemporary with him.
Henry: cut off at forty-seven, Joash might have reigned longer; it is a great mercy for young princes to be under good direction and willing to be ruled by it.
Clarke outlines the chapter: a good reign under Jehoiada, the temple repaired, Hazael bought off, the king slain by his servants.

outliers.note: Treats 'forty years' as a common round number, comparing the forty-year reigns assigned to David and Solomon.

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott defend the rendering 'all his days wherein Jehoiada instructed him' — righteousness lasting only while the priest lived — against Thenius's lifelong reading that would discredit the chronicler.
Gill states the substance: Jehoash worshipped the only true God and ruled and walked according to his law.
Clarke: upright while Jehoiada lived, then idolatry and a share in Zechariah's murder — 'O how few of the few who begin to live to God continue unto the end!'

debates.question: Does 'all his days wherein Jehoiada the priest instructed him' limit the king's obedience to Jehoiada's lifetime?

sides.position: yes — his righteousness lasted only while Jehoiada lived, as Chronicles' 'all the days of Jehoiada' correctly explains
the words can only mean 'all his life long, because Jehoiada had instructed him' — an ambiguity used to discredit the chronicler

schools.summary: Keil marks the standing formula of Kings; Ellicott renders 'save that' and notes the high-place worship continued even under Jehoiada's régime.
Gill: the people clung to the old altars for their antiquity, supposed sanctity, and ease; not even the best kings thought it safe to remove them before Hezekiah.
Henry: the altars honoured the God of Israel only, though in rivalry with Jerusalem; perhaps Jehoiada connived at them in hope reform would make them dwindle.
Clarke: without total destruction no radical reform; tolerated idolatry is a seed that regrows the whole infernal system, and Jehoiada failed to use his influence.

debates.question: Why were the high places left standing under Jehoiada?

sides.position: Jehoiada failed to use the influence he plainly had; toleration of any idolatry re-seeds the whole system
connivance or prudence — well-meaning worshippers in unsafe times, hope that temple reform would draw them off, and the danger of assaulting so inveterate a usage

schools.summary: Keil sorts the fund into census money, valuation money, and free-will offerings; Ellicott reads 'money that passeth' as current silver by weight, there being no coinage before the exile.
Gill: Joash repairs the house chiefly as the Lord's sanctuary, but also because it had been a sanctuary to him, hiding him six years.
Henry: the king was first and forwardest — God requires those with power to use it for religion's support, and the temple had been Joash's nursery and sanctuary.
Wesley: the guardian of the temple remembers his debt to it; Clarke details the three revenue sources and charges the priests with converting the income to their own use.

debates.question: What is 'the money of every one that passeth the account'?

sides.position: the census half-shekel, redemption money of the mustered (Exodus 30)
'current money' — silver of full weight for immediate payment to the workpeople

schools.summary: Ellicott compares Kings with Chronicles on how the priests collected, notes 'acquaintance' occurs only here, and blames the breaches on Athaliah's sons, not age.
Gill: the priests, with cities through the land, collected where they were best known, both legal dues and free gifts.
Henry: the sons of Athaliah had broken up God's house; and from 'every man of his acquaintance' he draws the duty of exciting our own acquaintance to what is good.
Ellicott: the order perhaps given in the king's twentieth year, at his coming of age; noticeably the king, not Jehoiada, takes the initiative, and 'the Levites hastened it not.'
Gill lists the possible faults — a backward people, priests converting the money, or plain negligence in collecting and applying it.
Henry weighs priestly carelessness against popular distrust, allows the plea of the priests' scanty maintenance, and rues that churchmen of all men should be slow at church work.
Clarke blames the priests' inactivity and Jehoiada's want of active zeal — simple piety without zeal effects no reformation, as Melancthon without Luther.

debates.question: Where did the fault of the twenty-three-year delay chiefly lie?

sides.position: the inactivity and negligence of the priests, and Jehoiada's want of active zeal
fault divided — priestly carelessness, popular distrust, or revenues absorbed by the priests' own scanty maintenance

outliers.note: Digresses into the Reformation — ten thousand pious but inactive Jehoiadas and Melancthons could not do what one flaming, consuming Luther did.

schools.summary: Keil: the king took from the priests both the collection and the duty of repair; Ellicott finds the account unclear, the priests reluctant to part with revenues they thought their own, the king's words throwing suspicion on them.
Gill distinguishes the summons: Jehoiada the high priest together with the common priests.
Henry commends the king for reproving even his tutor, yet asks whether Joash loved the service of the temple as much as the structure.

themes: the fund taken from the priests

schools.summary: Keil: the consent shows the king's command meant relieving the priests of both collection and repair; Ellicott: they doubtless explained themselves against the suspicion of fraud, then resigned the business.
Gill: they consented to receive no more money — and delivered up what they had.
Henry: it was honestly done that, wanting spirit for the work themselves, they did not hinder others from doing it.
Ellicott: a locked chest fed at once by the Levitical doorkeepers obviated all suspicion; Chronicles' 'in the gate outwards' probably reflects the chest's post-exilic station.
Gill: the chest was made by the king's commandment, to prevent any fraud or suspicion of any.
Henry: a public chest drew cheerful abundance; the gift dropped past recall teaches that what is resigned to God must never be resumed.
Clarke harmonizes the two positions: first beside the altar, afterwards set without the gate for the people's convenience.

debates.question: How do the chest's two recorded positions — beside the altar (Kings) and 'in the gate outwards' (Chronicles) — relate?

sides.position: the chest was moved: first beside the altar, later outside the gate for convenience
the notices can hardly describe the same position; Chronicles reflects where the chest stood after the Captivity

themes: the chest with the bored lid

schools.summary: Ellicott: whenever the chest was full the royal secretary and high priest bound, counted, weighed, and sealed the silver into bags that passed as money; Ewald prefers Chronicles' 'they emptied.'
Gill: the fulness of the chest was easily guessed from the number of contributors.
Henry: the high priest and the secretary of state counted the money and laid it by in specie for its appropriated use.
Clarke: civil authority had to be associated with the high priest to get the neglected work performed.
Keil: weighed money went to the appointed overseers, and the embezzlement charge of Michaelis and De Wette is perfectly imaginary; Ellicott: the recipients are superintendents, not workmen.
Gill: the overseers were laymen, not priests; Kimchi thought the money 'told' was coined before delivery to pay the workmen.
Henry: money belongs in the hands of men versed in such affairs — every artist has his trade, and the holy warfare must not be entangled in the affairs of this life.

debates.question: Had the priests embezzled the collected money?

sides.position: the charge (Michaelis, De Wette) is 'perfectly imaginary' — a king suspecting embezzlers would not have asked their consent
at least a diversion of revenues to their own support — the king's words throw suspicion on them

themes: every calling to its own work

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the trades as standing guilds, 'hewed stone' as quarry stone, and iterative tenses of repeated disbursement; Keil renders the whole stream of expenditure.
Gill: the hewers cut stone out of the mountain, with which the masons repaired the walls.
Henry: the business was done carefully — with expedition, because done by men who understood it.
Ellicott: the same three vessel-terms as in Solomon's inventory; the plain meaning is that the whole amount went to restoring the fabric.
Gill identifies the bowls of silver as vessels for the blood of sacrifices or the drink offerings of wine.
Henry: necessary repairs before ornaments — in all expenses give the preference to what is most needful, and for the public deal as for ourselves.
Clarke harmonizes with Chronicles: no vessels till the outward repairs were completed, then the rest of the money was made into vessels of gold and silver.

themes: the whole fund to the fabric

schools.summary: Ellicott renders the iterative 'they used to give... and repair,' and suspects Chronicles' surplus-vessels notice of being an edifying addition, though possibly drawn from the source.
Gill: they took care that the workmen should first be paid for the repairs.
Henry: the overplus was honestly turned into plate only after the repairs were finished — wages before ornaments.
Clarke takes the Chronicles sequel at face value: vessels of gold and silver made from the residue once the repairs were done.

debates.question: Is the chronicler's notice that surplus money became temple vessels historical?

sides.position: the natural sequel — repairs first, then the overplus turned into plate
it has the appearance of an edifying addition, though possibly found in the chronicler's source

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'they were not wont to reckon' — habitual trust in men above suspicion; no covert thrust at the priests, for the notice recurs under Josiah where priests are not concerned.
Gill: the secretary and high priest never audited the overseers nor examined how the money was laid out.
Henry: their reputation for honesty made audits needless; all entrusted with public money must deal faithfully, for God will reckon whether men do or no.
Clarke: great confidence placed and not disappointed; Wesley: the confidence was earned, 'for they perceived by many experiments that they were faithful.'

Keil grounds the trespass-money in the law but denies any legal sin-money, rejecting the common account as without trace; Ellicott: the new arrangement parted sanctuary gifts from priesthood gifts without robbing the priests.
Gill: money sent from a distance for trespass and sin offerings bought the sacrifices, and the remainder stayed with the priests, outside the repair fund.
Henry: the priests' stated maintenance was untouched — let not the servants of the temple be starved under colour of repairing its breaches.

debates.question: What was the 'sin money' left to the priests?

sides.position: money sent by distant worshippers for their offerings, the surplus falling to the priests (the common view)
the law gave the priests no sin-offering money, and of any such custom 'there is not the slightest trace'

schools.summary: Ellicott frames the chronology and Gath's status as Judah's western outpost, citing the Assyrian annals; Keil reconciles Kings and Chronicles into one campaign against Thenius.
Gill: with Jehoiada dead and Jehoash apostate, the Lord suffered the king of Syria to be a scourge to him, beginning with Gath.
Henry: Hazael threatened the royal and holy city 'whose defence, on account of its sinfulness, had departed.'
Clarke: Gath had been Judah's since David; Hazael needed no pretext — 'he had the ultima ratio regum, power to do it, and he wanted more territory.'

debates.question: Do Kings and Chronicles record one Syrian campaign or two?

sides.position: one campaign — the two brief accounts reconcile and supplement each other
two distinct expeditions, the accounts not to be harmonized
holders: Thenius (as reported by Keil and Ellicott)

schools.summary: Ellicott: even Baal-friendly kings had dedicated conciliating gifts; the temple treasure was a national reserve, and the bribe here explains the withdrawal Chronicles records.
Gill: the hallowed things of three kings, dedicated to sacred uses and laid up in the temple, were sent away with all the gold of both houses.
Henry's four-fold audit: forfeited protection, diminished honour, an impoverished kingdom, and a spoiler tempted to return.
Clarke: a peace dearly bought and short-lived — the treasures gone, the next invasion cost Joash his nobility, his kingdom's strength, and soon his life.
Gill: the rest of the acts stood in the registers and annals of the kings of Judah, some still to be met with in the canonical Chronicles.
Henry turns the epitaph into a mirror: a sad conclusion to a promising beginning should make us search our spiritual declinings and desire to know nothing but Christ.
Clarke fills the formula's silence: apostasy, idolatry, the murder of Zechariah — and God visiting that blood by Syria's king and the king's own servants.
Keil and Ellicott find Beth-Millo and Silla perfectly obscure — not the castle Millo, and 'Silla' probably a corruption — the wounded king slain on his bed by his attendants.
Gill sets the sequence: after the slaying of Zechariah and a second Syrian expedition that left the king diseased.
Henry: the conspirators sought revenge, not the crown; vengeance was not theirs, yet God was righteous — the blood of prophets runs the account very high.
Clarke identifies Beth-Millo as David's palace and Silla its causeway; Wesley: the provocation was the prophet's murder — Joash began in the spirit and ended in the flesh.

debates.question: What and where were Beth-Millo and Silla?

sides.position: David's royal palace between the old city and the city of David, Silla the causeway leading to it
perfectly obscure — not the castle Millo (always 'the Millo'), and 'Silla' probably a textual corruption of 'Millo'

schools.summary: Ellicott corrects the connective and preserves Thenius's observation that the assassins' names answer Zechariah's dying cry for remembrance and requital.
Gill notes the variants: Jozachar is Chronicles' Zabad, and his mother Shimeath an Ammonitess.
Clarke: both assassins sons of foreign mothers; the king buried in the city of David but not the kings' sepulchres, the people's disapprobation of an apostate reign.

outliers.note: Preserves Thenius's 'curious coincidence' that the assassins' names answer Zechariah's dying cry — Jozachar 'Jehovah remembers,' son of Shimeath 'hearing'; Jehozabad 'Jehovah bestows,' son of Shomer 'watcher.'
